title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
How Does Divorce Impact Your Term Insurance?
Marriages bring two people together, and they share everything that they have. Even in terms of financial possessions, the assets are usually considered to be joint possessions. However, sometimes, a marriage doesn’t go as planned, and couples choose to separate.
Divorce is both an emotional and a financial challenge. As the couple split their marriage, they also split their financial assets. In such cases, one often wonders what happens to a term insurance plan.
So, let’s understand.
Divorce and term insurance
A term insurance plan does not get impacted in the case of a divorce. If a spouse has bought the policy on his/her own life and made the other spouse the beneficiary or the nominee, the same can be changed post-divorce.
For instance, say Mr Sharma buys a policy and nominates his wife as the nominee. When the couple is divorced, Mr Sharma can change the nominee from Mrs Sharma to his children or parents.
Similarly, if Mr Sharma has named Mrs Sharma as the beneficiary, he can change the beneficiary to his children or parents after a divorce.
However, in the case of the irrevocable beneficiary, no change can be affected post-divorce. As the name suggests, irrevocable means something that cannot be revoked. So, once you name a beneficiary as an irrevocable beneficiary, the beneficiary cannot be changed. In such cases, even after divorce, the spouse will remain the irrevocable beneficiary in the life insurance policy if so named.
So, if Mr Sharma had named Mrs Sharma as the irrevocable beneficiary, and then they divorced, Mrs Sharma would remain the beneficiary of the policy throughout the tenure.
The Married Women’s Property Act, 1874¹
The Married Women’s Property Act of 1874 was enacted to safeguard the property of wives after their husband’s passing. The MWP Act clause is available when buying a term insurance policy. The husband can opt for the MWP clause and name his wife and/or children as the beneficiary of the policy. If opted, the benefit of the term plan goes solely to the named beneficiary. It separates the term plan from the rest of the estate of the husband and creditors, or other family members cannot lay claim to the plan’s benefits.
So, if the husband has opted for the MWP Act in the term plan and named his wife as the beneficiary, divorce would not impact the plan. The wife will continue to remain the beneficiary even after a divorce.
Managing the policy after a divorce:
After a divorce, here are some tips that couples can follow in their life insurance plans –
If it is a joint life policy, it is better to continue the policy till the chosen tenure. Term plans usually do not have a surrender benefit if you discontinue premium payment during the policy tenure. So, you might lose the benefit of the coverage and the premiums paid so far.
However, if the plan offers a surrender value, even then, surrendering is a bad idea since the value will be very small.
In the case of individual term plans, the spouse can change the nominee or the beneficiary detail, provided the other spouse is not the irrevocable beneficiary.
If the spouse has bought the policy to insure the other spouse, then divorce does not impact the plan. The spouse paying the premium can simply discontinue the payment, and the coverage will lapse.
The financial goals might change after a divorce. So, use the term plan calculator to assess your changing coverage needs. In the case of a shortfall, you can buy a new plan to supplement the coverage.
The bottom line
Divorces can be tough, but your term insurance policy needn’t be. Know what happens to the coverage after a divorce and make the changes to retain your financial asset even after a split.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article is not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. full_pdf_text:
How Does Divorce Impact Your Term Insurance?
Marriages bring two people together, and it may be considered as an association where they share everything that they have. However, sometimes, a marriage doesn’t go as planned, and couples choose to separate.
Divorce is both an emotional and a financial challenge. As the couple split their marriage, they may also split their financial assets. In such cases, one often wonders what happens to a term insurance plan.
So, let’s understand.
Divorce and term insurance
A term insurance plan does not get impacted in the case of a divorce. If a spouse has bought the policy on his/her own life and made the other spouse the beneficiary or the nominee, the same can be changed post-divorce.
For instance, say Mr Sharma buys a policy and nominates his wife as the nominee/beneficiary. When the couple is divorced, Mr Sharma can change the nominee from Mrs Sharma to his children or parents. However, if the policy was bought under the The Married Women’s Property Act, 1874, this may not hold true. Lets read further to understand this better.
The Married Women’s Property Act, 1874¹
The Married Women’s Property Act of 1874 (MWP Act) was enacted to safeguard the property owned by a women from in-laws, relatives, and creditors after their husband’s passing. One can buy a term insurance policy under MWP Act. The husband can opt for the MWP clause and name his wife and/or children as the beneficiary of the policy. If opted, the benefit of the term plan goes solely to the named beneficiary. It separates the term plan from the rest of the estate of the husband and his creditors, or other family members cannot lay claim to the plan’s benefits.
So, if the husband has opted for the MWP Act in the term plan and named his wife as the beneficiary, divorce would not impact the plan. The wife will continue to remain the beneficiary even after a divorce.
Managing the policy after a divorce:
After a divorce, here are some tips that couples can follow in their life insurance plans –
● If it is a joint life policy, it is preferred to continue the policy till the chosen tenure. Term plans usually do not have a surrender benefit if you discontinue premium payment during the policy tenure. So, you might lose the benefit of the coverage and the premiums paid so far. ● The financial goals might change after a divorce. So, use the term plan calculator to assess your changing coverage needs. In the case of a shortfall, you can consider buying a new plan to supplement the coverage.
The bottom line
Know what happens to the coverage after a divorce and make the changes to manage your financial asset even after a split.
Disclaimer: The views stated in this article is not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.
